title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Spot Prices Witnessed a WoW Rise; California LCFS Overhaul Kicks Off This Month
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(23rd June â€“ 29th June)
  • A total of 399,639 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 2.11x times rise week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a 1.97x times rise WoW as 1,486,002 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $43.17/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 4.55% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 2.44% in price, reaching $47.18/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 4.57% WoW; on 30th June, the spot price stood at $49.00/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $51.50/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 5.15% WoW. D4 and D5 RIN prices decreased by 11.11% WoW each. D6 RIN prices fell by 9.56% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

cCarbonâ€™s Market View CARB has confirmed that the amended Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) regulation is taking effect from July 1, 2025. CARB claims in its recent press release that it aims to drive $68B in revenue for California businesses and generate $4 B annually in clean transportation investments. The revised rule sets steeper carbon intensity (CI) reduction targetsâ€”30% by 2030 and 90% by 2045â€”while improving air quality, expanding EV charging access, and cutting healthcare costs by an estimated $12B, as stated by CARB. Contrary to fears of fuel price hikes, CARB notes gasoline prices have actually fallen, reflecting greater market efficiency and competition from cleaner alternatives. Following months of regulatory back-and-forth, CARBâ€™s announcement brings long-awaited certainty to the LCFS credit market. Spot credit prices, which had plunged to historic lows in June, rebounded by 8.25% on June 27, hitting $50 as the market responded to the upcoming stricter CI benchmarks. The RSI on benchmark prices, which was in the oversold territory (<30) between May 20 and June 12, crossed into the overbought zone (>70) on June 27. The Relative Strength Index (RSI) is a momentum indicator that measures the speed and change of price movements. It ranges from 0 to 100 and helps identify whether an asset is overbought or oversold. With credit demand likely to increase in the second half of 2025, market watchers will be eyeing the impact of these rules, especially as the Assembly considers SB-237, which could rework the LCFS credit price cap structure established in 2016. For detailed analysis of this, please read our article here.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_599780" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 4.55% WoW rise, increasing from $41.29 to $43.17 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 2.44% WoW, from $48.36 to $47.18. Type 1 trades saw 399,639 credits exchanged, reflecting a 2.11x times rise WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a 1.97x times rise WoW, reaching 1,486,002 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_599772" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 4.57% WoW, reaching $46.93/MT. On 30th June, the spot price was $49.00/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $51.50/MT as of 30th June.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_599773" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 122 trades were reported in the market, with 15 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $85.00 for one Type 2 transaction at a volume of 15,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $39.50 per credit of Type 1 reported for 10,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 93,105 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $40.50, and the smallest volume transfers of 34 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $43.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_599774" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $44.25 in June, down from $54.76 in May. Trade volumes have been on a consistent decline since April, with May recording 5.09 MMT and June's volume falling to 4.34 MMT as of June 29.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_599775" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 23rd Â June â€“ 29th June, total VPT recorded a 15.28% decline WoW, with a fall in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 5.15% WoW. D4 and D5 RIN prices decreased by 11.11% WoW each. D6 RIN prices fell by 9.56% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • California Assembly Committee Considers SB 237: LCFS Credit Price Cap, Fuel Blend Overhaul, and â€œOne-Stop Shopâ€ Refinery Permitting Gain Traction: "On 23rd June 2025, California assembly committee considered SB 237 . The bill proposes a cap on LCFS credit pricesâ€”set to the average market rate as of January 1, 2025, with future increases tied to inflation. The bill requires CARB to adopt these changes as emergency regulations exempt from traditional review processes, valid until January 1, 2031. The legislation also mandates standardized regulatory impact analyses for all CARB or Energy Commission rules that affect retail fuel prices.
  • Senate Passes Bipartisan Legislation to Jumpstart Sustainable Aviation Fuel Industry in Michigan: The Michigan Senate passed bipartisan legislation to help launch the sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) industry in Michigan. Sponsored by Senate Majority Floor Leader Sam Singh (D-East Lansing) and Sen. Joseph Bellino (R-Monroe), Senate Bills 235â€“36 would establish an income tax credit for the producers and blenders of SAF â€” a move that would support Michiganâ€™s farmers and economy while reducing the stateâ€™s carbon footprint. By providing a $1.50 per gallon tax credit for those who purchase, produce, or blend sustainable aviation fuel, this legislation would directly support farmers and the businesses that develop and supply cleaner-burning, ethanol-derived aviation fuels.
  • Hearing EIB 25-23(R): In the Matter of Proposed Adoption of 20.2.92 NMAC â€“ Clean Transportation Fuel Program: The New Mexico Environmental Improvement Board (â€œEIBâ€) will hold a public hearing beginning on September 22, 2025, at 9:00 am through October 3, 2025, resuming at 9:00 am on November 17, 2025 and continuing as long as required to hear all testimony, evidence, and public comment, regarding the proposed New Mexico Clean Transportation Fuel Standard.
  • CARB Announces Latest LCFS Updates Will be Implemented Next Month: CARB announced that the implementation of Californiaâ€™s amended LCFS will begin on July 1, 2025, following approval by the Office of Administrative Law. The amended regulation focuses public and private sector investment towards increasing cleaner fuel and transportation options for Californians, accelerating the deployment of zero-emission infrastructure, and helping the state achieve legally mandated air quality and climate targets. â€œImplementing the July 1 effective date for the LCFS provides critical certainty to industry, as well as the LCFS credit market,â€ said CARB Board Chair Liane Randolph. â€œBut often lost in the noise around this program are our primary reasons for approving it: better health for Californians, our economy and the environment, as well as achieving required state and federal air quality standards. "
